questions.question: Explain the three main types of I/O techniques used in computer systems.
keyPoints: Programmed I/O - CPU continuously polls device status
Interrupt-driven I/O - Device notifies CPU when ready
DMA - Device transfers data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention

questions.question: What is the memory hierarchy? Explain why it exists.
keyPoints: Hierarchy: Registers → Cache → Main Memory → Secondary Storage
Purpose: Balance speed, capacity, and cost
Trade-off: Faster memory is more expensive and has less capacity

questions.question: Describe the role of the bootstrap program in computer system organization.
keyPoints: First program executed on power-on
Initializes hardware components
Loads operating system into main memory
Stored in ROM/EEPROM

questions.question: What are the three types of system buses? Explain their functions.
keyPoints: Data Bus: Carries actual data between components
Address Bus: Carries memory addresses from CPU
Control Bus: Carries control signals (read/write, interrupt)

questions.question: Explain how interrupt-driven I/O improves system efficiency over programmed I/O.
keyPoints: CPU does not waste time polling device status
CPU can perform other tasks while waiting for I/O
Device notifies CPU only when ready
Reduces CPU overhead and improves responsiveness

questions.question: What is a multiprocessor system? List its advantages over a single-processor system.
keyPoints: System with multiple CPUs/processors
Advantages: Higher throughput, better reliability, improved scalability
Allows parallel processing of tasks

questions.question: Explain the concept of hardware protection in operating systems.
keyPoints: I/O Protection: Prevents unauthorized access to I/O operations
Memory Protection: Isolates processes memory spaces
CPU Protection: Uses timer to prevent process monopolization

questions.question: Explain the organization of a computer system and how the operating system interacts with hardware components. Discuss the OS as an abstraction layer.
keyPoints: Computer system components: CPU, Memory, I/O devices
System bus connects components
OS provides services: process management, memory management, file management, I/O management
OS abstracts hardware complexity from users and applications
OS manages resources efficiently: CPU scheduling, memory allocation, device scheduling
Examples of abstraction: device drivers hide device details, file system abstracts disk storage

questions.question: Discuss the various I/O techniques in detail, comparing their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Programmed I/O: Simple but CPU intensive, polls continuously
Interrupt-driven I/O: Efficient, device notifies CPU, but interrupt overhead
DMA: Best for large transfers, reduces CPU burden, but complex and expensive
Comparison table: CPU involvement, speed, hardware cost, suitable for devices

questions.question: Explain the memory hierarchy in computer systems. Why is cache memory important and how does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: Levels: Registers (fastest) → Cache → Main Memory → Secondary Storage (slowest)
Cache stores frequently accessed data/instructions
Temporal and spatial locality principles
Cache hit/miss concepts
Improves performance by reducing memory access latency
Trade-off between speed and cost/capacity

questions.question: What are interrupts? Explain the interrupt handling mechanism and its importance in computer system organization.
keyPoints: Interrupt definition: Signal requiring immediate attention
Types: Hardware (external), Software (internal/trap)
Handling: Save CPU state → Identify source → Execute ISR → Restore state
Importance: Enables asynchronous events, improves CPU utilization
Examples: I/O completion, arithmetic overflow, system calls
